Complete a full pre-trip inspection before operating any commercial vehicle.
Check brakes, lights, tires, steering, and couplings before departure.
Confirm there are no air, oil, or fuel leaks on the unit.
Inspect air brake lines and connections for damage or leaks.
Verify air pressure and warning devices function properly.
Do not operate with critical safety defects.
Wear your seat belt correctly at all times while driving.
Increase following distance when fully loaded.
Reduce speed before curves, ramps, and long downgrades.
Select proper gear before beginning a long downhill section.
Use controlled braking to prevent brake fade.
Scan mirrors frequently to monitor no-zones and lane traffic.
Signal early for all lane changes and turns.
Avoid abrupt lane changes that can destabilize the load.
Keep extra side clearance in urban and work-zone areas.
Adjust speed to weather, visibility, and pavement conditions.
Do not exceed posted limits, especially with heavy loads.
Verify cargo securement before departure and at required stops.
Balance load distribution to reduce rollover risk.
Secure doors, gates, and load restraints before moving.
Plan route for bridge heights, weight limits, and turn space.
Before backing, get out and check the entire backing area.
Back slowly and continuously verify path through mirrors.
When possible, use driver-side backing for better visibility.
Do not rely on unclear signals from others; verify safe path yourself.
Account for trailer off-tracking on tight turns.
Do not swing excessively left before a right turn.
Control trailer path carefully at narrow intersections.
Avoid sudden steering with high center-of-gravity loads.
With liquid cargo, drive smoothly to reduce surge effects.
Brake earlier than you would in a passenger vehicle.
Observe warning signs for grades, hazards, and special restrictions.
Use required warning devices when stopped on roadway shoulders.
Do not block emergency lanes or railroad crossings.
At railroad crossings, slow and confirm clear passage.
Avoid shifting while crossing tracks when possible.
In fog, reduce speed and increase following distance.
On snow or ice, avoid abrupt braking and steering.
Clear windshield, mirrors, and lights before winter operation.
Check wipers and defroster before starting in cold weather.
Do not drive fatigued; follow rest requirements.
If drowsy, stop in a safe location before continuing.
Never operate under alcohol, drugs, or impairing medication.
Keep required documentation available and current.
Report mechanical defects per company and regulatory procedure.
Complete post-trip inspections and note all defects.
Do not return unsafe vehicles to service before repair.
When entering highways, accelerate smoothly to merge safely.
When exiting, decelerate in the proper lane.
Avoid lingering in the blind spots of other large vehicles.
Anticipate cut-ins and merging conflicts from smaller vehicles.
Use horn only when it clearly improves safety.
Give extra space when passing motorcycles and bicycles.
Pass only with enough room to return without cutting off traffic.
Do not pass where visibility or signs prohibit it.
Readjust mirrors after driver changes or load changes.
Confirm landing gear, fifth wheel, and locks are secure.
Before moving a combination, verify full coupling engagement.
Check parking brake hold before exiting the cab.
Use wheel chocks where procedure requires them.
Monitor dual tire condition and inflation to prevent failure.
Investigate unusual vibration immediately.
Maintain controlled speed on long descents.
Avoid continuous heavy service braking on steep grades.
With tall or heavy loads, slow further in curves and crosswinds.
In strong wind, make smooth corrections and reduce speed.
When parking, choose positions with safe exit and visibility.
Do not stop where emergency access is blocked.
In yards and terminals, obey internal routes and speed limits.
Keep the cab organized to reduce distraction.
Do not handle devices while the vehicle is moving.
Use checklists to keep inspections consistent.
Communicate clearly with spotters during maneuvers.
Before opening cargo doors, confirm load has not shifted dangerously.
Maintain safety margin to avoid emergency maneuvers.
Scan far ahead for signals, congestion, and conflicts.
Never assume others will protect your space; drive defensively.
In school or residential zones, reduce speed and watch for pedestrians.
Plan alternate routes for closures, crashes, or severe weather.
Re-check securement after initial miles and as required.
Monitor brake temperature and performance on mountain routes.
Use long-range visual scanning to identify hazards early.
Do not force tight maneuvers; reposition when needed.
Follow special cargo procedures according to training and rules.
Use predictable professional driving behavior to reduce conflict.
If a maneuver is unsafe, stop, reassess, and restart under control.
Prioritize stability, visibility, and space management over speed.
Take review breaks when weather, load, or route conditions change.
